text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo
Attending Oncologist: [Attending Physician]
Department: Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with known Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma presenting as an urgent visit following restaging CT imaging obtained at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 100 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily. Imaging is notable for progressive disease, and she is presenting today to discuss these findings, her new symptoms, and next-line treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is well known to our clinic. As a brief review: she originally presented with Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) superficial spreading melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, with bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm). Molecular profiling at diagnosis confirmed a BRAF V600E mutation (VAF 48%), concurrent PTEN p.R130Q mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and high tumor mutational burden at 42 mutations/Mb. PD-L1 TPS was 45%. LDH was elevated at 387 U/L at the outset.

Given the rapid disease kinetics and high tumor burden at presentation, she was initiated on first-line dabrafenib/trametinib combination targeted therapy, which produced a prompt and robust response: at 8-week restaging, she had a dramatic reduction in target lesions -- right flank subcutaneous nodule regressed from 1.4 cm to 0.3 cm, bilateral axillary nodes reduced from 2.1 and 1.8 cm to 0.7 and 0.5 cm respectively, and her hepatic lesions were markedly smaller. LDH normalized. By the 6-month restaging scan, we had documented her best response with approximately 68% reduction from baseline and no new lesions. Her quality of life had improved substantially.

Unfortunately, she has now developed symptomatic and radiographic disease progression. Beginning approximately 3-4 weeks ago, she began to notice a new, intermittent right upper quadrant aching discomfort, which she describes as a dull heaviness rather than sharp pain, sometimes worse after eating a full meal. She also reports mild dyspnea on exertion -- specifically, she notices it climbing a full flight of stairs and during her outdoor work activities, though she denies dyspnea at rest. She has lost approximately 6 lbs over the past 4 weeks without deliberate dietary restriction, which she found alarming given she had worked hard to regain weight after initial diagnosis. She denies nausea, vomiting, change in bowel habits, hemoptysis, or hematemesis. She denies fever or chills. No new skin lesions or subcutaneous masses that she has noticed. No headaches, visual changes, neurological symptoms, or seizures.

She remains on dabrafenib 100 mg BID (which was dose-reduced from 150 mg BID approximately 4 months ago due to a grade 2 episode of pyrexia requiring ED evaluation, with concurrent management of grade 2 peripheral edema) and trametinib 2 mg daily. She reports good adherence to her oral medications and has not missed significant doses. She does note that the prior sense of energy improvement she had experienced in the first few months has gradually waned over the past 6-8 weeks.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed prior to this visit demonstrated: right axillary lymph node growth from 0.5 cm to 1.6 cm, a new left axillary lymph node measuring 1.1 cm, hepatic lesion growth in the right lobe (from 1.0 cm to 2.3 cm and from 0.5 cm to 1.4 cm), an entirely new right hepatic lobe lesion at 0.8 cm, and -- of note -- two new small pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe (0.6 cm and 0.4 cm) representing new organ involvement that was not present even at original staging. MRI brain obtained concurrently was reassuringly negative for intracranial metastases. LDH today is 512 U/L, which is substantially above her nadir of 178 U/L during treatment response.

This constellation of findings -- re-growing nodal and hepatic disease, new pulmonary lesions, rising LDH, and new symptoms -- is consistent with acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi therapy, which unfortunately we anticipated as a risk given the PTEN p.R130Q co-mutation identified at diagnosis, as PTEN loss has been associated with PI3K/AKT pathway activation as an escape mechanism from MAPK pathway blockade.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant unintentional weight loss of 6 lbs over 4 weeks. Fatigue, worse over the past 6-8 weeks but not debilitating. Denies fever or night sweats.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea as above. No cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or orthopnea.
Gastrointestinal: Right upper quadrant dull aching discomfort as above. Good appetite though mildly reduced. Normal bowel frequency and consistency. Denies nausea, vomiting, melena, or hematochezia. No jaundice noted by patient.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain, arthralgias, or myalgias.
Neurological: Denies headache, visual changes, focal weakness, numbness, seizure activity, or cognitive changes.
Skin: No new pigmented lesions noted by patient. Managed skin surveillance ongoing with dermatology.
Endocrine/Other: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine 100 mcg (dose increase implemented at prior visit). Denies palpitations, heat/cold intolerance.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM

Vitals: Temperature 37.2 degrees C; Heart rate 84 bpm; Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg; Respiratory rate 16; O2 saturation 97% on room air; Weight [recorded in chart]. ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Conversational. Mild temporal wasting compared to her prior visit.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae, mucous membranes moist, no oral lesions. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.
Lymph Nodes: Right axillary lymphadenopathy palpated -- a firm, mobile, non-tender node approximately 1.5-2 cm. Left axilla: a smaller, palpable but less prominent node. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema today (resolved from prior episode).
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No accessory muscle use. No wheeze or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild right upper quadrant tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin -- I do note slight hepatomegaly compared to our last examination, consistent with the interval hepatic lesion growth. No splenomegaly appreciated. No ascites.
Skin: Well-healed surgical scar at left posterior shoulder (site of primary resection). No new suspicious pigmented lesions on gross exam. No active rash; the prior forearm maculopapular rash has resolved.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal deficits on screening exam.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC 7.4 x10^9/L (normal differential)
  - Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable)
  - Platelets 198 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 25 mEq/L
  - BUN 16 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, eGFR >60
  - Glucose 92 mg/dL
  - Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL
  - ALT 78 U/L (mildly elevated, likely hepatic metastasis-related), AST 65 U/L (mildly elevated)
  - Alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (upper limit of normal, new mild elevation)
  - Albumin 3.7 g/dL

LDH: 512 U/L (elevated; reference range <250 U/L) -- significant rise from nadir of 178 U/L and above level at original diagnosis of 387 U/L.

TSH: 4.2 mIU/L (acceptable on current levothyroxine dose)

Coagulation studies: PT/INR, aPTT within normal limits

Hepatitis B surface antigen: Negative (confirmed prior to initiating immunotherapy, reviewed today)
Hepatitis B surface antibody: Positive (immunity)
Hepatitis C antibody: Negative

Baseline pre-immunotherapy labs drawn today:
  - Cortisol (AM): 18.2 mcg/dL (adequate baseline)
  - TSH: 4.2 mIU/L (as above)
  - Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
  - ACTH stimulation test deferred at this time given adequate AM cortisol

---

IMAGING RESULTS (REVIEWED TODAY)

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (obtained prior to this visit, approximately 8 months from initiation of dabrafenib/trametinib):

The details are reviewed in full. In summary: compared to the 6-month scan that showed her best response, we are now seeing unequivocal growth and new lesions across multiple sites:
  - Right axillary lymph node has grown from 0.5 cm to 1.6 cm, now again clearly pathologic in size.
  - A new 1.1 cm left axillary lymph node has appeared.
  - The two previously documented right hepatic lobe lesions have grown substantially -- from 1.0 cm to 2.3 cm and from 0.5 cm to 1.4 cm -- and there is an entirely new 0.8 cm right hepatic lobe lesion that was not present at the 6-month scan.
  - Most concerning is the appearance of two new right lower lobe pulmonary nodules measuring 0.6 cm and 0.4 cm. Even at her initial PET-CT staging, there was no pulmonary disease, so these represent new organ involvement and signify M1d disease evolution in the pulmonary compartment.
  - No new subcutaneous nodules, no pleural effusion, no peritoneal disease.

MRI Brain with and without gadolinium: No intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal enhancement, no new findings. This is reassuring.

Overall assessment: This is clear-cut disease progression after a period of durable response to targeted therapy. The pattern -- re-growth at prior sites plus new hepatic and now pulmonary lesions -- is consistent with acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi, the development of which is unfortunately anticipated in the majority of patients within 6-12 months despite initial response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma with acquired resistance to first-line dabrafenib/trametinib -- DISEASE PROGRESSION

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has developed progressive disease after approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib combination therapy. This is a challenging but anticipated clinical development. Her initial response was impressive -- approximately 68% reduction at best response -- and the duration of control (8 months from start) is within the range expected for this regimen in patients with high-burden, BRAF-mutant melanoma.

Mechanistically, acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi is multifactorial. In her case, the PTEN p.R130Q mutation identified at baseline is relevant -- PTEN loss activates PI3K/AKT signaling, which can serve as a bypass mechanism to MAPK pathway inhibition and has been linked to reduced durability of response. Additionally, CDKN2A homozygous deletion may contribute to cell cycle dysregulation independent of BRAF pathway blockade.

Given the clear progression on BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy, and considering her overall molecular profile -- BRAF V600E-mutant with high TMB (42 mutations/Mb) and PD-L1 TPS of 45% -- she is an excellent candidate for checkpoint inhibitor therapy. There is strong evidence from CheckMate-067 and subsequent long-term follow-up data supporting the combination of ipilimumab plus nivolumab as a highly active regimen in metastatic melanoma, including in patients with prior BRAF-targeted therapy. In the sequencing debate, her high TMB and meaningful PD-L1 expression suggest solid potential for durable immunotherapy benefit.

Plan: Initiate ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction doses, per the CheckMate-067 lower-dose ipilimumab schema. This regimen is chosen to balance efficacy with an intention to reduce immune-related toxicity burden compared to the standard 3 mg/kg ipi / 1 mg/kg nivo dosing, particularly relevant given her hepatic involvement and prior hepatic transaminase elevations. Following 4 induction doses, plan to transition to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as flat-dose maintenance. Standard pre-medications (diphenhydramine, acetaminophen) will be given with each infusion.

Dabrafenib and trametinib will be discontinued today. There is no role for continued targeted therapy in the setting of clear progression, and there are washout considerations prior to immunotherapy initiation. We will allow approximately 1 week of washout before starting first immunotherapy infusion.

2. Immune-Related Adverse Event Risk Counseling

An extensive counseling session was conducted today with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and her partner, who accompanied her to this visit. We discussed the following in detail:
  - Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) are a significant class effect of checkpoint inhibitor therapy and differ fundamentally from the side effects of chemotherapy or targeted therapy.
  - The most common serious irAEs include immune-mediated colitis/diarrhea, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, adrenal insufficiency, hypophysitis), and dermatitis.
  - With combination ipi/nivo, the rate of grade 3-4 irAEs is approximately 55-60% in published trials, substantially higher than with nivolumab alone.
  - She was counseled to contact our clinic immediately or go to the emergency department if she develops: more than 3-4 loose bowel movements per day above her baseline (or any bloody diarrhea), fever, skin rash, shortness of breath, significant fatigue, or visual changes.
  - Patient was provided with an immunotherapy toxicity wallet card and our after-hours contact number.
  - She verbalized clear understanding of the warning signs and agreed to present promptly for evaluation.
  - Steroid use for irAEs and its implications (including risk of adrenal insufficiency with prolonged use) were discussed.
  - Specifically regarding her prior hepatic transaminase elevations, she was told that immune hepatitis can occur and we will be monitoring LFTs closely -- every 2-3 weeks during induction.

3. Laboratory Monitoring Plan

Prior to each immunotherapy infusion: CBC with differential, CMP (specifically AST, ALT, total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, creatinine), LDH, and thyroid function (TSH with reflex free T4) every 4-6 weeks.

Baseline AM cortisol documented today at 18.2 mcg/dL -- adequate. Will repeat if symptoms of adrenal insufficiency emerge.

Hepatitis B serology confirmed negative for active infection -- safe to proceed with immunotherapy.

4. Hypothyroidism

Currently on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily (dose increased at prior visit from 75 mcg). TSH today 4.2 mIU/L -- mild, acceptable. Will recheck TSH at 6-week mark. Checkpoint inhibitor therapy may further destabilize thyroid function, and she is already at higher baseline risk given prior levothyroxine dependence. Endocrinology curbside discussed; formal referral will be placed if TSH becomes difficult to manage.

5. Symptom Management

Right upper quadrant discomfort: Attributed to hepatic capsule stretch from growing hepatic metastases. Acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain. Avoid NSAIDs in setting of mild transaminase elevation and in anticipation of immunotherapy (can complicate GI toxicity assessment). If pain escalates significantly, reassess for additional analgesic support.

Weight loss and nutritional status: Dietitian referral placed. Encouragement of high-calorie, high-protein diet. Will reassess albumin and weight trend at next visit.

Mild dyspnea: Given the small pulmonary nodule size (0.6 and 0.4 cm), the current exertional dyspnea is unlikely to be directly attributable to the pulmonary lesions at this size. Will continue to monitor. Baseline pulse oximetry at rest is 97% on room air -- reassuring. We will watch closely for any worsening pulmonary symptoms during immunotherapy given risk of immune pneumonitis.

6. Performance Status and Goals of Care

ECOG PS 1 today. Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has articulated clearly that her goals remain focused on disease control and quality of life, and she is committed to pursuing active treatment. She understands this is a next-line regimen and that her disease course has been challenging, but she remains engaged and optimistic. Her partner is a strong support.

Goals of care conversation was revisited briefly -- she remains interested in all active treatment options and does not wish to pursue hospice evaluation at this time, which is consistent with her PS and treatment options available. A palliative care co-management visit may be considered in the future for supportive care optimization.

7. Follow-Up

First ipi/nivo infusion scheduled in approximately 1 week (following dabrafenib/trametinib washout). LFTs and CBC to be rechecked 2 weeks after first infusion. Clinic visit scheduled at week 3 (prior to cycle 2). Restaging imaging planned at approximately 10-12 weeks (after cycle 3-4), per standard practice for immunotherapy response assessment.

Patient and partner verbalized understanding of the plan, questions were addressed, and they expressed agreement to proceed with combination immunotherapy.

---

IMPRESSION:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma who has developed acquired resistance to dabrafenib/trametinib after approximately 8 months of therapy with prior best partial response of ~68%. She has both symptomatic and imaging evidence of progressive disease, with new pulmonary lesions representing new organ involvement. She has a favorable immunotherapy profile (PD-L1 45%, TMB high at 42 mut/Mb) and is ECOG PS 1 with no intracranial disease. Plan to discontinue targeted therapy and initiate second-line combination ipilimumab/nivolumab (CheckMate-067 schema: ipi 1 mg/kg + nivo 3 mg/kg q3w x4 doses, then nivo 480 mg q4w maintenance). Thorough irAE counseling performed. Close monitoring of hepatic function, thyroid function, and symptom burden planned.

Cancer status: Present, progressive disease.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Dictated and reviewed by Attending Oncologist]
